title: Listing outcomes using a tree diagram
problem: A bag contains one red (R) marble and one blue (B) marble. A marble is drawn at random, replaced, and then a second marble is drawn. Draw a tree diagram and list all outcomes in the sample space.

steps.instruction: Draw the first stage of the tree.
steps.working: From the start, draw two branches — one labelled R and one labelled B.

steps.instruction: Draw the second stage of the tree.
steps.working: Since the marble is replaced, the same two options (R and B) are available for the second draw. From each first-stage branch, draw two more branches labelled R and B.

steps.instruction: List all outcomes by following each path from start to end.
steps.working: The four paths give outcomes: RR, RB, BR, BB.

title: Finding probability from a tree diagram (with replacement)
problem: A bag contains 2 red (R) and 1 blue (B) marble. A marble is drawn, replaced, then drawn again. Using a tree diagram, find $P(\text{at least one blue marble})$.

steps.instruction: Write probabilities on the first-stage branches.
Write probabilities on the second-stage branches.
steps.working: Because the marble is replaced, the probabilities are the same for the second draw. Write $P(R) = \dfrac{2}{3}$ and $P(B) = \dfrac{1}{3}$ from each first-stage branch.

steps.instruction: Multiply along each path to find the probability of each outcome.
Add the probabilities for all outcomes that include at least one blue marble.
steps.working: $$P(\text{at least one blue}) = P(RB) + P(BR) + P(BB) = \frac{2}{9} + \frac{2}{9} + \frac{1}{9} = \frac{5}{9}$$

title: Finding probability from a tree diagram (without replacement)
problem: A bag contains 3 red (R) and 2 blue (B) marbles. Two marbles are drawn without replacement. Using a tree diagram, find $P(\text{one red and one blue})$.

steps.instruction: Write probabilities on the first-stage branches.
Write probabilities on the second-stage branches. These change because the first marble is not replaced.
steps.working: If first draw was R: 2 red and 2 blue remain from 4 total, so $P(R) = \dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ and $P(B) = \dfrac{2}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$. If first draw was B: 3 red and 1 blue remain from 4 total, so $P(R) = \dfrac{3}{4}$ and $P(B) = \dfrac{1}{4}$.

steps.instruction: Find the probability for each mixed-colour path.
